index.title: Resource Translator
index.description: Open machine-translated pull requests for resource files in any repo, in any format.
index.badge: v3.0 — Node 24, repo config, glossary, dry run
index.lede: Resource Translator opens machine-translated pull requests for your resource files using Azure AI Translator via the official SDK — no extra services, just a workflow.
index.workflowTitle: Translation as a workflow, not a chore.
index.workflowBody: Drop the action into your existing CI, point it at Azure AI Translator, and let your repo speak every language you target.

config.title: Repo-level config
config.body: Drop a .github/resource-translator.yml for include globs, glossaries, and Custom Translator categories.

glossary.title: Glossary-aware
glossary.body: Lock brand names and product terms with smart word-boundary regex that handles C++, .NET, and friends.

dryRun.title: Dry-run + soft-fail
dryRun.body: Preview translations without writing files, or surface every error as a warning while you iterate.

outputs.title: PR-friendly outputs
outputs.body: summary-title, summary-details, and has-new-translations slot directly into create-pull-request.

sdk.title: Official Azure SDK
sdk.body: Built on @azure-rest/ai-translation-text + @azure/core-rest-pipeline — automatic retries on 408/429/5xx, no hand-rolled HTTP. esbuild-bundled, audit-clean.

getting-started.title: Getting started
getting-started.lede: Add a workflow file, point it at your Azure AI Translator credentials, and the action — built on Microsoft's official @azure-rest/ai-translation-text SDK — will scan the repo for source-language resource files and emit translated siblings.

provision.title: Provision an Azure AI Translator resource
provision.body: Create an Azure AI Translator resource in the Azure portal, then store the following as GitHub repository secrets so the workflow can read them:
provision.key: the subscription key for the resource.

resource.title: Commit a source resource file
resource.body: Resource files use the Name.<sourceLocale>.<ext> convention. For example Greetings.en.resx becomes Greetings.fr.resx, Greetings.de.resx, and so on after a run.

output.title: Inspect the output
output.body: Once the workflow finishes, the action exposes three outputs you can wire into a PR description: summary-title, summary-details, and has-new-translations. It also writes a Markdown summary into the job page via core.summary.

nextSteps.title: Next steps
nextSteps.recipes: See recipes for monorepos, custom glossaries, and Custom Translator categories.

configuration.title: Configuration
configuration.lede: Express defaults once in .github/resource-translator.yml and let every workflow inherit them. Action inputs always win when both are provided.

inputs.title: Inputs & outputs
inputs.lede: Every input is also accepted in the optional repo config file. When both are set, the action input wins.

formats.title: File formats
formats.lede: Six round-trippable resource formats. The action reads each source-locale file, translates the values, and writes a sibling per target locale.

recipes.title: Recipes
recipes.lede: Common patterns for shipping translations: monorepos, glossaries, custom categories, dry runs, and tone control.

faq.title: FAQ
faq.lede: Quick answers to the questions that come up most often when adopting the action.

translations.title: Translations
translations.filterPlaceholder: Search by language, code, or native name…
translations.translationPending: Translation pending — open a translate-docs run.

contributing.title: Contributing
contributing.lede: Bug reports, feature requests, and pull requests are all welcome. The action is small enough to be friendly to first-time contributors.

changelog.title: Changelog
changelog.lede: High-level summary of releases. The canonical, authoritative changelog lives in CHANGELOG.md in the repository.
